profile.name: Tobiloba Awolaju
profile.title: Web3 Systems Engineer • Smart Contract Developer • Multiplayer Game Architect
profile.subtitle: Building on-chain games, DeFi protocols, and real-time decentralized applications.
profile.bio: I build full-stack Web3 systems across smart contracts, realtime backends, wallet-connected frontends, and browser-based game clients. My strongest work sits at the intersection of blockchain settlement, multiplayer infrastructure, and product engineering — the kind of systems where the frontend, backend, contracts, and player/user experience all have to stay consistent.

engineeringHighlights: Built a 20-player real-time multiplayer Web3 game
Smart contract development and protocol architecture
currentlyBuilding: Research into scalable on-chain multiplayer systems

projects.title: World of Nads
projects.description: 20-player browser battle royale with real-time multiplayer, smart contract settlement, WebSocket backend, wallet integration, and a Godot/WebAssembly client embedded in a React product experience.
bullets: Built with React, Godot, Node.js, Solidity

details.Overview: A no-install, in-browser Web3 battle royale that combines a Godot game client, React wallet onboarding, realtime WebSocket gameplay, and on-chain prize distribution.
details.Problem: Most blockchain games feel either like websites with tokens attached or games with blockchain bolted on after the fact. World of Nads needed realtime gameplay while still settling outcomes on-chain.
details.Architecture: React handles product UI and wallet flow, the Godot client runs in-browser through WebAssembly, a Node.js WebSocket server keeps player state synchronized, and Solidity contracts handle settlement.
details.Technical Challenges: Keeping 20 players synchronized under network jitter while avoiding expensive per-frame bridge calls between Godot, React, wallet state, and backend state.
details.Solutions: Rebuilt the sync path around batched state updates after profiling a bottleneck that dropped browser FPS from 60 to under 12.
details.Tech Stack: React, TypeScript, Godot, WebAssembly, Node.js, WebSockets, Solidity, PostgreSQL, Privy.
details.Screenshots: worldofnads1.gif, worldofnads2.gif, worldofnads3.gif, worldofnads4.gif, worldofnads5.gif, worldofnads6.gif
details.Architecture Diagrams: Wallet + React UI → Godot WASM Client → Node.js WebSocket Server → Solidity Settlement Contracts.
details.Lessons Learned: Realtime Web3 products only feel credible when game state, wallet state, backend state, and contract state are treated as one system instead of separate features.

projects.title: Rug Pool
projects.description: A memecoin launch protocol with bonding curves, verifiable randomness, Solidity contracts, Foundry/Hardhat testing, and Monad deployment work.

details.Overview: A memecoin launchpad where no one — not even the deployer — can exit early; on-chain randomness determines the exit outcome after the lock period.
details.Problem: Memecoin launch mechanics often rely on trust, admin behavior, or off-chain coordination. Rug Pool explores hard contract-level constraints for a launch game with explicit risk.
details.Architecture: The frontend coordinates launch and participation flows, Solidity contracts enforce locks and payout rules, and verifiable randomness selects the exit outcome.
details.Technical Challenges: Enforcing a hard lock without an admin backdoor while keeping randomness unpredictable and independently verifiable.
details.Solutions: Used Pyth Entropy for VRF and validated payout logic with contract tests and simulation cycles already described in the project notes.
details.Tech Stack: Solidity, Foundry, Hardhat, Monad, TypeScript, React.
details.Architecture Diagrams: React App → Protocol Contracts → VRF Randomness → Monad Settlement.
details.Lessons Learned: Protocol UX is strongest when the rules are legible in the interface and impossible to bypass in the contracts.

projects.title: MoyakiTraceEngine
projects.description: A reorg-aware block indexer that cross-references independent Monad nodes and rolls back state on parent-hash mismatch.
